annotations.label: Improper integral

anchor.snippet: an improper integral is an extension of the notion of a definite integral

annotations.note: Mathlib has no top-level `ImproperIntegral` definition; the AECover framework and `intervalIntegral_tendsto_integral_Ioi` provide the underlying machinery.

annotations.label: First, second, and third kind

anchor.snippet: The first three forms are improper because the integrals are taken over an unbounded interval

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the textbook three-kind classification of improper integrals.

annotations.label: Improper integral as a limit (case 1)

anchor.snippet: the improper integral must be rewritten using one or more limits

annotations.note: `intervalIntegral_tendsto_integral_Ioi` (and the `Iic` version) expresses the integral on `Ioi a` as the limit of definite integrals, exactly the improper-integral-as-limit definition.

annotations.label: Improper integral with interior discontinuity

anchor.snippet: Here both limits must converge to a finite value for the improper integral to be said to converge

annotations.note: Splitting at an interior singularity is not a named concept, but the AECover formalism can express such two-sided limits.

annotations.label: Improper integral with discontinuity at endpoint

anchor.snippet: with a discontinuity of any kind

annotations.note: Endpoint singularities are handled implicitly through the AECover/Ioi-limit framework rather than as a dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Integral of 1/x^2 on [1,∞)

anchor.snippet: the Riemann integral can often be extended by continuity

annotations.note: `integral_Ioi_rpow_of_lt` evaluates `∫ x in Ioi c, x^a` for `a < -1`, which covers `a = -2`, `c = 1`.

annotations.label: Integral with unbounded integrand on [0,1]

anchor.snippet: The problem here is that the integrand is unbounded in the domain of integration

annotations.note: The specific `∫₀¹ x^{-1/2}` example is not stated, but `integrableOn_Ioi_rpow_iff` and related lemmas cover its convergence behavior.

annotations.label: Doubly improper integral of 1/((x+1)√x)

anchor.snippet: Thus this is a doubly improper integral

annotations.note: This specific doubly improper example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Divergent improper integrals of 1/x and 1/√x

anchor.snippet: a limit might fail to exist, or might be infinite

annotations.note: `not_integrableOn_Ioi_rpow` shows `x^s` is never integrable on `Ioi 0`, capturing the divergence of `1/x` and `1/√x`.

annotations.label: Splitting at an interior singularity

anchor.snippet: an integrand is unbounded near an interior point, in which case the integral must be split at that point

annotations.note: Interval-additivity of integrals lets one split at an interior point, but the improper-splitting protocol is not a named lemma.

annotations.label: Convergence of an improper integral

anchor.snippet: An improper integral converges if the limit defining it exists

annotations.note: Convergence is captured via integrability on `Ioi a` together with `intervalIntegral_tendsto_integral_Ioi`.

annotations.label: Divergence to infinity

anchor.snippet: It is also possible for an improper integral to diverge to infinity

annotations.note: Mathlib does not have a named concept of an improper integral diverging to `+∞`.

annotations.label: Divergence by oscillation

anchor.snippet: This is called divergence by oscillation

annotations.note: There is no `divergence by oscillation` concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Improper integral with two infinite endpoints

anchor.snippet: can be defined by taking two separate limits

annotations.note: AECover supports two-sided limit definitions (e.g. via `Ioc (-n) n`) but the textbook two-limit definition is not a named lemma.

annotations.label: Gaussian integral and infinite example

anchor.snippet: An example of an improper integral where both endpoints are infinite is the Gaussian integral

annotations.note: `integral_gaussian` proves `∫ x : ℝ, exp (-b * x^2) = √(π / b)`.

annotations.label: Cauchy principal value (two infinite endpoints)

anchor.snippet: one can however define an improper integral in the sense of Cauchy principal value

annotations.note: Cauchy principal value is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemann improper vs Lebesgue improper

anchor.snippet: often an integral which only exists as an improper Riemann integral will exist as a (proper) Lebesgue integral

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the Bochner/Lebesgue integral throughout; the comparison theorem itself is not stated.

annotations.label: Henstock–Kurzweil avoids improper integration

anchor.snippet: For the Henstock–Kurzweil integral , improper integration is not necessary

annotations.note: Although `BoxIntegral.IntegrationParams.Henstock` is defined, the principle that HK eliminates improper integration is not formalized.

annotations.label: Apostol Theorem 10.33

anchor.section: Improper Riemann integrals and Lebesgue integrals
anchor.snippet: If a function f is Riemann integrable on

annotations.note: Apostol's exact statement comparing Riemann integrability on compact subintervals with Lebesgue integrability is not present.

annotations.label: Properly improper integral

anchor.section: Improper Riemann integrals and Lebesgue integrals
anchor.snippet: Such cases are "properly improper" integrals

annotations.note: The notion of `properly improper` integrals is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Singularities of an improper integral

anchor.snippet: One can speak of the singularities of an improper integral

annotations.note: No singularity catalog for improper integrals exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cauchy principal value

anchor.snippet: The former is the Cauchy principal value of the otherwise ill-defined expression

annotations.note: Cauchy principal value is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Summability methods

anchor.snippet: These are called summability methods

annotations.note: General summability methods for integrals are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cesàro (C, α) summability of an integral

anchor.snippet: is Cesàro summable

annotations.note: Cesàro summability for integrals is not formalized; only a Cesàro-type asymptotic for sequences appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: (C,0) summability equals improper convergence

anchor.snippet: An integral is (C, 0) summable precisely when it exists as an improper integral

annotations.note: This equivalence is not in Mathlib since (C, α) summability of integrals is absent.

annotations.label: Grandi's series integral

anchor.snippet: This is an integral version of Grandi's series

annotations.note: No Grandi-series integral example is formalized.

annotations.label: Multivariable improper integral

anchor.snippet: The improper integral can also be defined for functions of several variables

annotations.note: AECover is stated in arbitrary measure spaces, but the multivariable Riemann improper integral as such is not formalized.

annotations.label: Improper integral over R^n via cubes

anchor.snippet: is a non-negative function that is Riemann integrable over every compact cube

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the Bochner integral; the multivariable Riemann-on-cubes construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Extension by zero outside A

anchor.snippet: is extended to a function

annotations.note: `integrable_indicator_iff` characterizes integrability of the zero-extension via the indicator function.

annotations.label: Riemann integral over a bounded domain

anchor.snippet: The Riemann integral of a function over a bounded domain

annotations.note: `BoxIntegral` provides multivariable Riemann-style integrals over boxes, but the textbook 'integrate the zero-extension over a containing box' presentation is not given.

annotations.label: Improper Riemann integral over arbitrary unbounded domain

anchor.snippet: if A is unbounded, then the improper Riemann integral over an arbitrary domain

annotations.note: The Riemann improper integral over an arbitrary unbounded domain via exhaustion by cubes is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Improper integral via truncation at M

anchor.snippet: the improper integral of f is defined by truncating f at some cutoff

annotations.note: The truncate-at-cutoff-then-take-limit definition is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Positive and negative part decomposition

anchor.section: Functions with both positive and negative values
anchor.snippet: A more general function f can be decomposed as a difference of its positive part

annotations.note: `posPart_sub_negPart` formalizes `f = f⁺ - f⁻`; `posPart`/`negPart` are defined throughout the order/Lp library.

annotations.label: Absolute convergence of improper Riemann integral

anchor.section: Functions with both positive and negative values
anchor.snippet: the improper integral necessarily converges absolutely

annotations.note: `integrable_norm_iff` shows Bochner integrability is equivalent to integrability of `‖f‖`, but the Riemann-improper version of the article's claim is not stated.
